title: Tori in Positive Characteristic
speaker: Ananth Shankar
abstract: I will discuss questions pertaining to geometric unlikely intersections and transcendence in the setting of torii in positive characteristic. This is based on work in progress joint with Anup Dixit, Philip Engel, and Ruofan Jiang.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic-p reformulation of Manin-Mumford
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the characteristic-zero Manin-Mumford statement for subvarieties of complex tori, then explains that over F_p-bar every point is torsion, so the meaningful replacement is density of positive-dimensional cosets rather than torsion points.

proof_stages.label: Dense-coset product theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: if an irreducible subvariety X of G_m^n contains a Zariski-dense set of positive-dimensional cosets, then after a GL_n(Z) change of coordinates it splits as G_m^a x X_1.

proof_stages.label: Primitive finite-field point test
proof_stages.summary: The speaker proposes a finite-field counting criterion: for X not contained in a subtorus, the normalized number of F_{q'}-points of exact order q'-1 should match the corresponding constant for the whole torus, with known upper bounds and examples supporting the prediction.

proof_stages.label: Full-rank étale monodromy theorem
proof_stages.summary: To support the sieve-style upper bounds, the speaker states that if X is not contained in a subtorus then the l-adic fundamental-group image in Z_l^n has full rank, and for large l is maximal/surjective; the proof sketch later uses finite covers and valuation relations.

proof_stages.label: Formal log Ax-Lindemann statement
proof_stages.summary: A companion theorem is formulated: although formal subgroups of the completed torus are Z_p-parametrized and hence more numerous than algebraic subtori, if the formal completion of X at the identity lies in a proper formal subgroup, then X lies in a proper algebraic subtorus.

proof_stages.label: Rational jets and their positive-characteristic finiteness
proof_stages.summary: The proof of the dense-coset theorem is organized through jets: algebraic cocharacters define rational jets, and in characteristic p cocharacters congruent modulo a sufficiently large p-power yield the same finite-order jet, making the relevant rational-jet space finite over the torus.

proof_stages.label: From dense cosets to algebraic torus factors
proof_stages.summary: Intersecting the jet space of X with rational jets and using finiteness shows that every point of X has compatible rational jets of all orders; p-adic limiting of cocharacters gives formal subtori in X, whose Zariski closures become algebraic subgroups and yield the product decomposition.

proof_stages.label: Local-to-global p-power argument for log Ax-Lindemann
proof_stages.summary: For the formal Ax-Lindemann theorem, the speaker treats a curve in G_m^3: approximate Z_p exponents by integers, upgrade local p^n-power relations in the completed function field to global ones, and use boundary valuations to force a rational linear relation, hence an algebraic subtorus.

examples.name: A curve times a one-dimensional torus
examples.note: Taking an arbitrary curve C in G_m^2 and forming C x G_m in G_m^3 shows that dense positive-dimensional cosets need not make X a subtorus, motivating the product-form conclusion.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs C×G_m inside G_m×G_m^2 and checks it has dense positive-dimensional cosets via {point}×G_m for points of the curve.

passes.reason: an ordinary product with a torus factor is used to refute the stronger expectation that dense positive-dimensional cosets force a subtorus
It is used to show that dense positive-dimensional cosets need not make X a subtorus, while the product with an arbitrary curve is structurally ordinary.

examples.name: Primitive points in the whole torus
examples.note: Computing the exact-order q-1 proportion in G_m^n gives the constant (1-p^{-n})/zeta(n), which is then used as the predicted benchmark for general X.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the primitive-point ratio to the whole torus G_m^n and identifies the resulting limiting constant, briefly explaining the missing p-part factor.
